title: Setting Up
description:
Welcome to the first chapter of The Legend of Python!

Python is a beginner-friendly programming language created by Guido van Rossum in the early 1990s. It is widely used in Artificial Intelligence, Web Development, Data Analysis, and Machine Learning.

Let's dive in right away!

print() function is use for output statements. 
In common scenario it is use in displaying messages.

Parentheses call the function followed by a statement.

Relax adventurer, we'll study this on the next exercises.
In this exercise, you will use print() to display your first message.
task: Run the code on the editor.
lesson_example:
Welcome to The Legend of Python!
Well done on your 1st exercise.
starting_code:
# PRESS THE RUN BUTTON

print("Welcome to The Legend of Python!")
print("Well done on your " + "1st exercise.")

title: Hello World
description:
Have you notice the output statement print()?
Yes, you're right! 
It is like English language.

Python is designed like that for better readability.
And is using new lines to complete a command.

Try this code on the editor:
  print("Hello,") print("World!")

This will result an error.
To fix it, you must put each print() in a separate line.
Try fixing it and run it again.

Here is a coding challenge for you adventurer!
To wrap up this exercise Gyahaha!
task:
Write a program that:

Prints your name on one line

Prints your age on another line

Use the print() function for both.

Prints your age on another line

title: Arithmetic Operations
description:
Gyahahaha!
Are you surprised in your previous exercise?
Yes, it is possible.

Arithmetic Operations are basic mathematical calculations in Python.
Using basic built-in operators. These include:

  Addition (+): 5 + 3 = 8

  Subtraction (-): 5 - 3 = 2

  Multiplication (*): 5 * 3 = 15

  Division (/): 6 / 3 = 2.0 
  (always returns a float)

  Floor Division (//): 7 // 2 = 3 
  (returns the quotient, rounded down)

  Modulus (%): 7 % 3 = 1 
  (returns the remainder)

  Exponentiation (**): 2 ** 3 = 8 
  (raises a number to a power)


Ready to explore?
What!? You want to program a calculator?
Just kidding. 

Here is your task.
task:
Using the print() function, display the result of the following operations:

5 + 8

20 - 12

4 * 12

15 / 3

7 // 2

7 % 3

2 ** 3

Each operation must be printed on a separate line.

title: Initials
description:
Let's wrap up everything.
But before that we will explore a little bit.

Try this code:
  print(("Gui" + "do ") * 3)

What do you notice? 
It joins "Gui" and "do" then print thrice.
Joining the String "Gui and "do" together is called concatenation.

The parentheses inside the print() function serves as a grouping expression just like the PEMDAS.
In Python it is called Operator Precedence.

Ready for a challenge?
Let's go!
task:
Concatenate the initials of your name and print them 4 times.

Use only one print() function.
Remember operator precedence… Gyahahaha!

title: Introduction to Variables
description:
Whatta noice badge you've got!

Let's not waste a precious time of yours adventurer!

Variables are named containers.
They are used to store data values.

Amazing, right? Ohh yeah here's an example:
  name = "Alex"
  age = 15
  bool = True

Gyahahaha! Now it's your turn adventurer.
Show your knowledge from your previous adventures.

Print the variables using the print() function.

title: Strings
description:
You're probably wondering…
As an adventurer, it is only natural to have a strong gut feeling.

And yes — you are absolutely right!

There are different types of variables.

Earlier, you saw something like this:

name = "Alex"


This is called a String variable, because it stores text.

The single equal sign = is used to assign a value to a variable.

So in the example above:

The variable is name

The value is "Alex"

The = connects them together

This means the variable name now stores the text "Alex".

Amazing, right?

But here’s something even more interesting…

A string variable does not always need to store fixed text.

It can also store text entered by the user!

Python allows us to do this using the input() function.

For example:

name = input("Enter your name: ")


Now, whatever the user types will be stored inside the variable name.

The variable is no longer fixed — it becomes dynamic!

Challenge Time, Adventurer!

Let the world know who you are!

title: Numbers
description:
Ahhh… I see that confident look in your eyes, adventurer.

You’ve mastered fixed numbers.
You’ve bent arithmetic to your will.
You’ve stored values like a true scholar of Codemania.

But tell me…

What if the numbers don’t come from you?

What if they come from the world itself?

Gyahahaha!

Until now, your numbers were fixed:

a = 5
b = 3


Predictable.
Stable.
Safe.

But a true adventurer does not fear the unknown.

The input() function allows you to receive values from the user.
However… beware!

input() returns text — not numbers!

If you try to add them without preparation, chaos will unfold:

"5" + "3" → "53"


That is not addition.
That is deception!

To convert text into real numbers, you must use the sacred spell:

int()


Example:

number = int(input("Enter a number: "))


Now the value becomes a true numeric variable,
ready for calculations.

title: Booleans
description:
In Codemania, truth is everything.

There are only two possible paths:

True

False

Nothing in between.
No maybe.
No perhaps.
Only truth… or falsehood.

These are called Boolean values.

When you assign:

isStudent = True


You are creating a Boolean variable.

Remember — Boolean values must be written with a capital letter:

True

False

If you write true or false…
Chaos will follow.

Gyahahaha!
task:
Challenge Time, Adventurer!

1. Create a Boolean variable named isStudent.

2. Assign it either True or False.

3. Print the variable using print().

Let Codemania know the truth!

Let Codemania know the truth!

title: If Statements
description:
Control flow…
It is how Codemania reacts to your decisions.

Until now, your code has executed line by line —
predictable… obedient.

But what if the program must decide?

The if statement allows your code to choose a path.

When a condition evaluates to True,
the code inside the if block runs.

For example:

age = 18

if age >= 18:
    print("Adult")


If the condition is true, the message appears.
If not… nothing happens.

Notice:

The comparison operator >=

The colon :

The indentation under the if

These are not optional.
They are the laws of Codemania.

title: Else & Elif
description:
The world of Codemania is not always simple.

Sometimes one condition is not enough.

What if the adventurer is not an adult?

Should the program stop?

No.

That is why we use:

if

elif

else

These create a chain of decisions.

But this time… the age will not be fixed.

The user will decide it.

Using input(), you can ask the adventurer for their age.

Remember…

input() returns text.

To compare numbers properly, you must convert it using:

int()


Only then can your logic work correctly.

Challenge Time, Adventurer!

2. Convert it into an integer.

3. Use an if-elif-else statement to categorize the age:

3. If age is 18 or older → print "Adult"

4. If age is 13 or older → print "Teen"

5. Otherwise → print "Child"

Choose wisely… the path depends on the number.

Gyahahaha!

title: Comparison
description:
Comparison allows you to measure the land…

To judge what is greater, smaller, equal… or not equal.

In Codemania, nothing moves without comparison.

You have learned how to store numbers.
You have learned how to receive input from the world.

Now it is time to compare them.

The > operator checks if one value is greater than another.

It returns a Boolean value:

True

or False

But remember…

When using input(), the value is returned as text.

To compare numbers properly, you must convert them using:

int()


Only then can the truth be revealed.

2. Convert both inputs into integers.

3. Compare the first number to the second using the > operator.

4. Print the result of the comparison.

Let Codemania decide which number is greater!

Gyahahaha!

title: Logical Operators
description:
Combining conditions…
Now that is true power.

You have learned how to compare values.
You have learned how to use if statements.

But what if one condition is not enough?

What if you must check multiple truths at once?

That is where logical operators come in.

Python gives you three powerful tools:

and → True if both conditions are True

or → True if at least one condition is True

not → Reverses a Boolean value

Example:

a = True
b = False

print(a and b)


This will print:

False


Because both conditions are not True.

Logical operators allow you to combine decisions…
and shape the fate of Codemania itself.

title: For Loops
description:
Repeating actions…
That is where true power begins.

Until now, your code ran once… and only once.

But what if you need to perform an action multiple times?

That is where the for loop comes in.

A for loop allows you to repeat a block of code
a specific number of times.

It often works together with the range() function.

Example:

for i in range(3):
    print(i)


This will print:

0
1
2


Why?

Because range(3) generates the numbers:

0, 1, 2

And the loop runs once for each number.

Notice carefully:

The keyword for

The word in

The function range()

The colon :

The indentation

These are the laws of repetition.

Challenge Time, Adventurer!

You must:

2. Use range()

3. Print the number inside the loop

Harness the power of repetition!

title: While Loops
description:
The for loop repeats a known number of times…

But what if you do not know how many times something should run?

What if repetition depends on a condition?

That is where the while loop rises.

A while loop continues running
as long as a condition is True.

Example:

count = 0

while count < 3:
    print(count)
    count += 1


This will print:

0
1
2


Notice carefully:

A starting variable (count = 0)

A condition (count < 3)

A colon :

Indentation

Updating the variable (count += 1)

Without updating the variable…
the loop will never stop.

And Codemania will fall into infinite chaos.

Gyahahaha!

Challenge Time, Adventurer!

title: Range Function
description:
You have learned that range() can control where a loop starts and stops.

But what if you don’t want to move one step at a time?

What if you want to move faster?

The range() function can take three arguments:

range(start, stop, step)


start → where the loop begins

stop → where it ends (exclusive)

step → how much it increases each time

Example:

for i in range(1, 6, 2):
    print(i)


This prints:

1
3
5


Notice…

It skips every second number.

This is called the step value.

With this, you control not only where you begin and end…
but how you move.

Powerful, isn’t it?

Gyahahaha!
task:

You must:

2. Use range(start, stop, step)

3. Start at 2

4. Stop at 11

5. Step by 2

6. Print inside the loop

Precision is power.

✅ Expected Output

4
6
8
10

title: Nested Loops
description:
Sometimes… one loop is not enough.

What if you must repeat something…
and inside that repetition,
repeat something else again?

That is where nested loops come in.

A nested loop is simply:

A loop inside another loop.

The inner loop completes all its iterations
for every single iteration of the outer loop.

Example:

for i in range(2):
    for j in range(2):
        print(i, j)


Let’s break it down:

Outer loop runs with i = 0

Inner loop runs fully (j = 0, j = 1)

Then outer loop moves to i = 1

Inner loop runs fully again

Output:

0 0
0 1
1 0
1 1


The outer loop controls rows.
The inner loop controls columns.

This is how grids, combinations, and patterns are built.

Powerful… isn’t it?

Gyahahaha!

title: The Program
description:
Every C++ program follows a specific structure so the compiler knows how to build and run it.

Look at that example code on the editor adventurer.

In line 1: #include <iostream> is a header file library to handle the input and output object, such as cout in line 5.

The line 2: using namespace std; lets us write cout instead of std::cout and we can use names for objects and variables from the standard library.

Every C++ program must have a int main() in line 3. 
It is called a function and any code inside its curly brackets {} is where execution begins.

Lint 4: cout is an object used together with the insertion operator << to output or print text. In our example, it will output "Hello adventurer!"

The statement return 0; in line 5 tells the main function ends. 

Understanding these parts is important because all C++ programs are built on this structure.

Every C++ statement ends with a semicolon ;.

title: Comments
description:
Did you notice this symbol // ? Gyahahaha!

It is called Comments.
Comments are used to explain code and make programs easier to understand.

They are ignored by the compiler and do not affect program execution.

Comments are especially useful when working on larger programs or when code needs to be read by others. 

C++ supports single-line comments using // and multi-line comments using /* */.

title: Variables
description:
Congratulations on getting your first badge!

Now we're gonna learn Variables.
Variables are used to store data values in memory.
It has data types that determines what kind of value it holds.

Such as: 
  int - stores integers whole number without decimal.
  double - stores floating point numbers with decimals.
  char - stores single characters. Its values are surrounded by single quotes.
  string - stores text. Its values are surrounded by double quotes.
  bool - stores values with two states: true or false.

Wanna try? You're really an adventurer! Gyahahaha!

Write these on the editor:
  string name = "Juan";
  int age = 23;
  double height = 5.4;

  cout << "Hello, I am " << name << age " years old.";
  cout << "I am " << height << " feet tall.";

title: Type Casting
description:
Now let's level up.

What happens when different data types meet each other?

Welcome to Type Casting.
Sometimes you need to convert one data type into another.
For example:
  int → double
  double → int
  char → int

Why? Because not all data types behave the same way!
Sometimes converts types automatically.
  
  int a = 5;
  double b = a;
  cout << b;

The int automatically becomes double.
Here, a becomes 5.0 automatically.

Easy, right?

Sometimes you must tell C++ to convert the type.

  double x = 9.7;
  int y = (int)x; 
  cout << y;

double converted to int. The output will be 9.
The decimal part is removed — not rounded!

Remember what you’ve learned about data types — they matter!

title: Arithmetic Operators
description:
You’ve learned about data types.
You’ve learned about type casting.

Now it’s time to do math.

They are symbols used to perform mathematical operations.
Here are the main ones:
  +  Addition  
  -  Subtraction         
  *  Multiplication
  /  Division 
  %  Modulus or remainder

Example Time! Write it on the code editor!
  int a = 10;
  int b = 3;

  cout << a + b;  // 13
  cout << a - b;  // 7
  cout << a * b;  // 30
  cout << a / b;  // 3
  cout << a % b;  // 1

Wait…
Why is a / b equal to 3 and not 3.33?

Because both a and b are integers!
Integer division removes the decimal part.

If you want decimals:
  cout << (double)a / b;  // 3.33333

NOTE: 
The % operator only works with integers.
It gives the remainder after division.

title: Comparison Operators
description:
Now let's make your program make decisions.
To do that, we need Comparison Operators.

They compare two values.
The result is always:
  true - 1
  false - 0

Here they are:
  ==	Equal to
  !=	Not equal to
  >   Greater than
  <   Less than
  >=	Greater than or equal to
  <=	Less than or equal to

Example:
  int a = 5;
  int b = 3;

  cout << (a > b) << endl;   // 1 (true)
  cout << (a < b) << endl;   // 0 (false)
  cout << (a == b) << endl;  // 0
  cout << (a != b) << endl;  // 1

Notice something???
We used parentheses ( ).

Why?

Because cout << a > b would confuse the compiler.
So always wrap comparisons inside parentheses when printing.

Reminder:
  =   means assignment
    int x = 5;   // assigning
  ==  means comparison
    x == 5;      // comparing

Using with user input.
Try this:
  int num;
  cout << "Enter a number: ";
  cin >> num;

  cout << (num > 10);

If the user enters 15, the output will be:
  1
Because 15 is greater than 10.

title: Assignment Operators
description:
Now it’s time to store and update values like a boss.
Welcome to Assignment Operators!

The simplest one you already know:
  int x = 5;   // assigns 5 to x

But there’s more! C++ gives shortcuts to update a variable without typing it all again.
=   - Assign
+=  - Add and assign
-=  - Subtract and assign
*=	- Multiply and assign	
/=	- Divide and assign
%=	- Modulus and assign

Examples:
  int a = 10;

  a += 5;   // a = 10 + 5 → 15
  cout << a << endl;

  a -= 3;   // a = 15 - 3 → 12
  cout << a << endl;

  a *= 2;   // a = 12 * 2 → 24
  cout << a << endl;

  a /= 4;   // a = 24 / 4 → 6
  cout << a << endl;

  a %= 4;   // a = 6 % 4 → 2
  cout << a << endl;

Shortcut operators make your code shorter and easier to read.
  x += 5 → “Add 5 to x and store it back in x.”

title: For Loop
description:
You’ve learned how to make decisions with if and switch. 
Now let’s make your program repeat things automatically.

Welcome to for loops in.

A for loop lets your program repeat a block of code a known number of times.

for (initialization; condition; update) {
    // code to repeat
}

Initialization - starting point (e.g., int i = 0)
Condition - loop continues while this is true
Update - what happens after each repetition (e.g., i++)

Example: Print Numbers 1 to 5
  for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
    cout << i << " ";
  }

  Output:
  1 2 3 4 5

Explanation:
  Start i = 1
  Check i <= 5 - true - run the loop
  i++ - add 1 to i
  Repeat until i > 5

Try this!
i++ - adds 1 each time
i-- - subtracts 1 each time
You can use any step: i += 2 to skip numbers

Try printing only even numbers from 1 to n using a step in the loop.

Hint:
  Use if statement.
  Use arithmetic operators.
  Use Logical Operators.

title: While Loop
description:
You’ve learned the for loop — perfect when you know how many times to repeat something.
But what if you don’t know in advance how many times you need to repeat?

That’s when the while loop comes to the rescue!
A while loop repeats a block of code as long as a condition is true.

while (condition) {
    // code to repeat
}

The condition is checked before each repetition.
If it’s true - the code runs.
If it’s false - the loop stops.

The condition is checked before each repetition.
If it’s true - the code runs.
If it’s false - the loop stops.

Example: Count from 1 to 5
  int i = 1;

  while (i <= 5) {
      cout << i << " ";
      i++;  // don’t forget to update!
  }

  Output:
  1 2 3 4 5

Notice: 
We initialized i before the loop and updated it inside.
Forget the update → infinite loop!

description:
Programs need a way to store information.

In JavaScript, we use variables to store data.

To create a variable, we use the keyword "let".

The equals sign = assigns a value to the variable.

The value can be a word, a number, or other types of data.

title: Arithmetic Operators
description:
Numbers are not just for storing values.

We can also perform calculations using mathematical operators.

In JavaScript, the basic arithmetic operators are:

+ Addition
- Subtraction
* Multiplication
/ Division

Example:

let a = 5;
let b = 3;

console.log(a + b);

The result will be:

8

JavaScript calculates the expression inside console.log() and displays the result.

You can perform calculations directly or use variables.

Mathematical operations are essential in programming.

title: Functions
description:
As programs grow, repeating code becomes inefficient.

Functions allow us to group code into reusable blocks.

A function is defined using the function keyword.

It has:

A name

Parentheses ()

Curly braces {}

Example:

function greet() {
  console.log("Hello!");
}

This defines the function.

But it does not run yet.

To execute the function, we must call it:

greet();

When called, the code inside the function runs.

Functions help organize code and avoid repetition.

They are a fundamental building block in programming.

title: Parameters
description:
Functions become more powerful when they can receive information.

This information is called a parameter.

A parameter is a variable listed inside the parentheses of a function.

Example:

function greet(name) {
  console.log("Hello " + name);
}

Here:

name is the parameter

It acts like a variable inside the function

When we call the function, we provide a value.
That value is called an argument.

greet("Sakura");

The output will be:

Hello Sakura

Parameters allow functions to work with different values.

This makes functions flexible and reusable.

title: Return Values
description:
So far, our functions print values.

But functions can also send values back.

This is done using the return keyword.

When a function returns a value, it sends the result back to where it was called.

Example:

function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

The function does not print anything.

Instead, it returns the result.

To see the value, we can store it in a variable or print it.

let result = add(5, 3);
console.log(result);

The output will be:

8

The return statement ends the function and gives back a value.

This allows functions to produce results that can be reused.

title: Arrays
description:
Sometimes, we need to store multiple values.

Instead of creating many separate variables,
we can store them inside an array.

An array is a collection of values.

Arrays are written using square brackets [].

Example:

let fruits = ["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"];

Each value inside the array is separated by a comma.

To access a value, we use its index.

Indexing starts at 0.

console.log(fruits[0]);

Output:

Apple

Arrays allow us to group related data together.

They are essential in programming.

title: Array Methods
description:
Arrays can do more than just store values.

JavaScript provides built-in methods to modify arrays.

A method is a function that belongs to an object.

Arrays have many useful methods.

One common method is:

push()

The push() method adds a new value to the end of an array.

Example:

let fruits = ["Apple", "Banana"];

fruits.push("Cherry");

console.log(fruits);

Output:

["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"]

The array now contains the new value.

Array methods allow us to modify and manage data easily.

title: Objects
description:
Sometimes, we need to store related information together.

An object allows us to store data using key–value pairs.

Objects are written using curly braces {}.

Each value is assigned to a key.

Example:

let person = {
  name: "Sakura",
  age: 18
};

Here:

name is a key

"Sakura" is its value

age is another key

18 is its value

To access a value, we use dot notation.

console.log(person.name);

Output:

Sakura

Objects help organize structured data.

They are widely used in JavaScript.

title: DOM Introduction & Selecting Elements
description:
The Document Object Model (DOM) is how JavaScript interacts with a webpage.

When a browser loads an HTML page, it creates a structured representation of the page in memory.
This structure looks like a tree of elements.

Each HTML element becomes an object that JavaScript can:

Access

Modify

Add

Remove

The entry point to this structure is the document object.

Using the document, JavaScript can find and control elements on the page.

Before you can change anything on a webpage, you must first select an element.

The method:

document.querySelector()

selects the first element that matches a CSS selector.

A selector can be:

#id → select by id

.class → select by class

tagname → select by element type

Example
const el = document.querySelector("#message");

This selects the element with id "message".

Selecting elements is the foundation of working with the DOM.

Store it in a variable named message.

title: Manipulating Content
description:
In Lesson 13 You learned how to select an element using:

document.querySelector()

Selecting an element allows JavaScript to access it.

Now that you can access an element, you can change its content.

To change the text inside an element, use:

element.textContent

The .textContent property allows you to:

Read the current text

Set new text

Example
const message = document.querySelector("#message");
message.textContent = "New Text";

This updates the text displayed on the page without reloading it.

This is one of the most important abilities of the DOM — updating what users see dynamically.

title: Manipulating Class
description:
In Lesson 13, you learned how to select elements using:

document.querySelector()

In Lesson 14, you learned how to change text using:

element.textContent

Now we will add another important skill: modifying classes.

The classList property allows you to add or remove CSS classes from an element.

To add a class:

element.classList.add("className")

Adding a class allows you to change how an element looks using CSS.

Now you will combine:

Selecting an element

Changing its text

Adding a class

title: Create and Append Element
description:
So far, you have: Selected elements using document.querySelector() Changed text using .textContent Modified classes using .classList.add() Now you will learn how to create new elements and add them to the page. JavaScript can dynamically build HTML elements using: document.createElement("tagName") This creates a new element in memory. To insert it into the page, use: parentElement.appendChild(newElement) Example const newParagraph = document.createElement("p"); newParagraph.textContent = "Hello World"; document.querySelector(".container").appendChild(newParagraph); This creates a new

element and adds it inside .container. This is how JavaScript dynamically builds content on a webpage.
